--- SOURCE 1: Burden of bacterial antimicrobial resistance in low-income and ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0140673624008626

SUMMARY:
Bacterial infections (excluding Mycobacterium tuberculosis) harbouring antimicrobial resistance (AMR) are estimated to be associated with approximately 5·0 million deaths annually, 1 with 4·3 million occurring in low-income and middle-income countries (LMICs).



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Authors

[Giorgia Sulis](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=Sulis+G&cauthor_id=34225545)[1](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34225545/#short-view-affiliation-1 "Department of Epidemiology, Biostatistics and Occupational Health, McGill University, Montreal, Canada."),[Sena Sayood](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=Sayood+S&cauthor_id=34225545)[2](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34225545/#short-view-affiliation-2 "Department of Internal Medicine, Division of Infectious Diseases, Washington University School of Medicine, St Louis."),[Sumanth Gandra](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=Gandra+S&cauthor_id=34225545)[2](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34225545/#short-view-affiliation-2 "Department of Internal Medicine, Division of Infectious Diseases, Washington University School of Medicine, St Louis.")

 Item in Clipboard 

 Full text links Cite

Display options

 Display options 

 Format 

Abstract
--------

**Introduction:** Rising rates of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) globally continue to pose agrave threat to human health. Low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) are disproportionately affected, partly due to the high burden of communicable diseases.

**Areas covered:** We reviewed current trends in AMR in LMICs and examined the forces driving AMR in those regions. The state of interventions being undertaken to curb AMR across the developing world are discussed, and the impact of the current COVID-19 pandemic on those efforts is explored.

**Expert opinion:** The dynamics that drive AMR in LMICs are inseparable from the political, economic, socio-cultural, and environmental forces that shape these nations. The COVID-19 pandemic has further exacerbated underlying factors that increase AMR. Some progress is being made in implementing surveillance measures in LMICs, but implementation of concrete measures to meaningfully impact AMR rates must address the underlying structural issues that generate and promote AMR. This, in turn, will require large infrastructural investments and significant political will.

**Keywords:** Antibiotic use; Antimicrobial resistance (AMR); Antimicrobial stewardship; Low- and middle-income countries (LMICs); Review.

[PubMed Disclaimer](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/disclaimer/)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Low-income countries face significant AMR challenges, with approximately 4.3 million annual deaths attributed to resistant infections, highlighting urgent health disparities.
High rates of AMR in low-income settings strain healthcare resources, leading to increased hospital admissions, prolonged treatments, and higher economic burdens on fragile healthcare systems.
Addressing AMR requires equitable solutions that ensure access to effective antibiotics and healthcare, particularly in resource-limited settings, fostering global health equity.
